name: Miner's Basin
short_description: BLM-managed rockhounding site near Moab offering agate and jasper collecting in a scenic high-desert basin. Free public access with standard 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Miner's Basin sits within Utah's colorful high-desert landscape, a region shaped by Mesozoic sedimentary rock formations and weathering processes that expose gem-quality agate, jasper, and chalcedony. This BLM land offers rockhounders straightforward public access without permits or fees, making it an excellent destination for both beginners and experienced collectors exploring the Grand County rockhounding circuit.

The basin terrain features rolling hills and wash systems typical of the Colorado Plateau, where ancient volcanic ash beds and silica-rich sediments have concentrating these semi-precious minerals over millions of years. Collectors typically find specimens in dry washes, eroded hillsides, and under loose surface material where weathering has naturally exposed colorful agate nodules and banded jasper pieces.

This site works well as part of a multi-location Grand County rockhounding trip. Moab sits just 30 miles away, providing full services and accommodations. The basin's moderate elevation (around 5,800 feet) means spring and fall weather is ideal, though summer heat can be intense and winter access may be limited by snow.

Because this is personal-use BLM land, you can collect freely within the 25 lb daily limit with no permit required. Popular among rockhounders seeking quality specimens without the commercial pressures of fee dig sites, Miner's Basin rewards patient searching with attractive multicolored agates and banded jasper suitable for tumbling, display, or lapidary work.
history: Miner's Basin lies within the Colorado Plateau, where Mesozoic sedimentary formations and Tertiary volcanic activity deposited silica-rich layers that weathered and concentrated into agate and jasper deposits. The basin's exposed geology reflects millions of years of erosion and mineralization typical of Utah's productive gem and mineral regions.
tips: Search dry washes and eroded hillsides where water runoff has exposed and concentrated agate nodules - these areas often yield the best specimens. Look for colorful banding patterns and translucent to waxy luster characteristic of quality agate; pieces ranging from golf ball to fist size are common finds. Bring a small hand pick and classifier screen to sift through loose gravels in wash systems efficiently. Early morning visits avoid afternoon heat and provide better light for spotting colorful specimens against the reddish-brown soil.
terrain_notes: Miner's Basin sits at approximately 5,800 feet elevation with gentle to moderate rolling hills, sparse desert scrub vegetation (sagebrush, scattered juniper), and multiple tributary washes. Terrain is open and mostly walkable but with uneven footing, scattered rocks, and loose material requiring sturdy boots. Most productive areas are within 1-2 miles of parking pullouts.
best_season: October - April. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees F with intense sun exposure and minimal shade. Late spring (April) and early fall (September-October) can work but are still quite warm. Winter may bring snow above 6,000 feet limiting access.
written_directions: From Moab, take US 191 north approximately 15 miles. Turn east on County Road 128 (Castle Valley Road) and follow it for about 10 miles into the basin area. Look for BLM parking pullouts marked along the road where you can safely park and access surrounding hillsides and wash systems. GPS coordinates 38.545647, -109.281621 mark the central basin area.
road_conditions: County Road 128 is unpaved but well-maintained, passable by standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Avoid after heavy rain or winter weather when washboard and mud make access difficult.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along County Road 128 serve as parking areas. Most are small (2-5 vehicle capacity) with no developed facilities. Shade is minimal. No fees or reservations required.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collecting with hand tools only (pick, chisel, screen). Pack out all material and leave site undamaged. Stay on public BLM land only.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
Hand tools only (pick, chisel, screen)
Pack out all material
Leave site undamaged
Stay on public BLM land
nearest_services: Moab, Utah (30 miles south). Full services including gas, food, water, lodging, and outdoor supply stores. Canyonlands National Park visitor services also nearby.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain with gentle slopes and open ground, but uneven footing and scattered rocks require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Most activity is short walking distances from parking pullouts (under 1 mile). Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or balance issues.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Miner's Basin near Moab?
faq.answer: Miner's Basin is known for colorful agate, jasper, chalcedony, and chert. Specimens range from small pea-sized pieces to fist-sized nodules. Many collectors find banded and multicolored agates suitable for tumbling or display. The site's geology produces quality material without the crowds of commercial fee dig sites.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Miner's Basin Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required at Miner's Basin. This BLM land allows personal recreational collecting under standard public land rules. You can collect freely with hand tools, limited to 25 lbs per day. No fees or advance registration needed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Miner's Basin?
faq.answer: October through April offers the best conditions. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees with minimal shade, making afternoon rockhounding uncomfortable. Winter snow above 6,000 feet can limit road access. Spring and early fall (April and September-October) are also viable but still warm - plan for early morning collecting.

faq.question: How far is Miner's Basin from Moab and what tools do I need?
faq.answer: Miner's Basin is approximately 30 miles north of Moab via US 191 and County Road 128. Bring a small hand pick, chisel, and sturdy boots with good grip. A classifier screen helps sift loose gravels in washes. No power tools or motorized equipment are allowed on BLM land.

faq.question: Is Miner's Basin rockhounding site beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Miner's Basin is excellent for beginners. Agate and jasper specimens are visible on the surface and in washes without deep excavation. The open terrain and straightforward collecting rules make it ideal for families and new rockhounders. Kid-friendly activity with specimens suitable for children ages 6 and up.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Miner's Basin Utah?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM public land. Keep your dog under control around the basin's washes and exposed cliffs. Bring plenty of water for both you and your pet - summer heat and remote location mean limited water sources. Avoid rattlesnakes that can be active in warm months.

meta_title: Miner's Basin Rockhounding - Free Agate & Jasper in Utah
